elements.sent-2411-001: Boundary layer diffusion, however, has historically hindered experimental study of this regime.
elements.sent-2411-002: Here, we utilize the boundary-independent oscillatory thermal-inertial mode of rotating convection to realize the diffusivity-free scaling in liquid metal laboratory experiments.
elements.sent-2411-003: This oscillatory style of convection arises in rotating liquid metals (low Prandtl number fluids) and is driven by the temperature gradient in the fluid bulk, thus remaining independent of diffusive boundary dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-004: We triply verify the existence of the diffusivity-free regime via measurements of heat transfer efficiency $\boldsymbol{Nu$, dimensionless flow velocities $\boldsymbol{Re}$, and internal temperature anomalies $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, all of which are in quantitative agreement with planar asymptotically-reduced models.}
elements.sent-2411-005: Achieving the theoretical diffusivity-free scalings in desktop-sized laboratory experiments provides the validation necessary to extrapolate and predict the convective flows in remote geophysical and astrophysical systems.
elements.sent-2411-006: Diffusivity-free (DF) turbulent convection is theorized to occur in the convection zones of stars and the fluid cores of planets, where it drives vortical flows, jets, and dynamo action that sustains global-scale magnetic fields \citep{moffatt2019self .}
elements.sent-2411-007: The DF regime is characterized by strong turbulence dominated by fluid inertia, largely unaffected by the fluid's micro-scale viscosity ($\nu$) and thermal diffusivity ($\kappa$).
elements.sent-2411-008: For decades, researchers in the fluid physics and geophysics communities have sought to validate this theoretical regime through measurements of heat transfer efficiency in numerical simulations and laboratory experiments \citep[e.g.,][]{ahlers2009heat, ecke2023turbulent .}
elements.sent-2411-009: It remains an essential question whether the theoretical DF regime can be attained in a laboratory setting and be extrapolated to natural settings.
elements.sent-2411-010: The canonical system for studying rotationally-constrained turbulent flows is the rotating Rayleigh-Bénard convection (RRBC) model \citep{ecke2023turbulent .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In RRBC, a fluid layer confined between two parallel no-slip boundaries is heated from below and cooled from above while rotating about its central vertical axis.
elements.sent-2411-012: Scaling laws that relate global heat transport (Nusselt number, $Nu$) and bulk flow velocity (Reynolds number, $Re$) to the buoyancy forcing (Rayleigh number, $Ra$), global rotation period (Ekman number, $E$), and material properties (Prandtl number, $Pr$) enable the extrapolation of laboratory-numerical findings to more extreme geophysical and astrophysical scales \citep[]{kraichnan1962turbulent, spiegel1971convection, stevenson1979turbulent, christensen2006scaling, plumley2019scaling, abbate2023rotating .}

elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{figures/experiment_main.png} \caption{Rotating laboratory setup. elements.sent-2411-013: The scaling predictions for the DF regime are derived by enforcing the constraint that thermal and viscous diffusion have no effect on the total heat transported through the system nor the internal flow velocities, and assuming that the system's angular rotation rate goes to infinity ($\Omega \rightarrow \infty$) \citep{julien2012heat .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The global heat transport prediction for rotating DF convection follows \begin{equation}Nu-1 = C_J \ Ra^{3/2} E^2 Pr^{-1/2}, \label{eqn:DF_Nusselt}\end{equation} where $C_J$ is a constant prefactor.
elements.sent-2411-015: This value was determined by \citet{julien2012heat to be $C_J\approx1/25$ in doubly-periodic planar models of the asymptotically reduced RRBC equations.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Scaling predictions for the internal quantities are determined from a torque balance between the inertial and buoyancy terms of the vorticity equation describing rotating flows, neglecting viscous diffusion \citep[e.g.,][]{ingersoll1982motion .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Assuming any scaling coefficients other than $C_J$ are order unity (see Methods), the prediction for the internal flow velocity is \begin{equation}Re = C_J^{2/5} Ro_c^2 E^{-1}, \label{eqn:DF_Reynolds}\end{equation} and for the internal temperature fluctuation is \begin{equation}\theta/\Delta T = C_J^{3/5} Ro_c, \label{eqn:DF_Theta}\end{equation} where $\theta$ is the root-mean-squared temperature perturbation, and $Ro_c = (RaE^2Pr^{-1 )^{1/2}$ is the convective Rossby number \citep[][and see Methods]{abbate2023rotating .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: These scalings have yet to be unambiguously found in any closed cell RRBC laboratory setup, thus raising the question of their applicability to any natural enclosed system \citep[cf.][]{soderlund2014ocean .}
elements.sent-2411-019: The fundamental barrier to achieving diffusion-free convection in RRBC lies in the physical boundaries through which heat is supplied and extracted.
elements.sent-2411-020: Boundary layers throttle energy transfer through the system.
elements.sent-2411-021: In most RRBC cases, the primary mode of convective instability is that of steady convection, which is thermally controlled by the conditions at the boundaries \citep{king2012thermal, julien2016nonlinear .}
elements.sent-2411-022: The critical value of the Rayleigh number for which steady convective motions first develop (denoted as $Ra_S$, see Methods) is determined from a balance between friction and the Coriolis and pressure gradient forces, such that the steady mode is considered a viscous convective instability \citep{zhang2017theory, aurnou2018rotating .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Consequently, heat transfer in typical steady RRBC is dominated by boundary layer diffusion \citep[e.g.,][]{abbate2023rotating, oliver2023small , which impedes the system from reaching the asymptotic DF scaling limit.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Lateral boundaries in RRBC give rise to additional sidewall-attached convective instabilities.
elements.sent-2411-025: These ``wall modes'' manifest as waves that travel azimuthally around the periphery of the tank, and are also a viscous instability \citep{ecke1992hopf .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Between contributions from steady and wall mode convection, RRBC heat transfer is often strongly influenced by diffusive effects.
elements.sent-2411-027: In order to mitigate boundary contributions in convection studies, researchers have employed innovative techniques to bypass the boundaries altogether.
elements.sent-2411-028: Numerically, this has been achieved through the use of ghost layers or forced decoupling of boundary layers \citep[]{barker2014theory, zou2021realizing .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Experimentally, radiative heat deposition directly into the fluid bulk \citep[]{lepot2018radiative, bouillaut2021experimental has recently found the asymptotic heat transfer scaling exponent, but a very different value of the prefactor than that of \citet{julien2012heat due to the differing means of thermal energy injection.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Their results may apply to the rotating convective turbulence present in gas planets and stars; however, it is uncertain that they apply to terrestrial fluid environments where convection is still driven by fluxing heat through finite, solid boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-031: Thus, bounded RRBC systems have yet to yield unambiguous asymptotic scalings, raising the dual concerns $i$) that standard laboratory-scale devices may be unable to realistically simulate geophysical turbulence or $ii$) that DF scalings do not exist in natural convection enclosed by solid boundaries \citep[]{favier2020robust, de2020turbulent, zhang2021boundary, lu2021heat, madonia2023reynolds .}
elements.sent-2411-032: To address these questions, we utilize the thermal-inertial oscillatory mode of convection that exists in low $Pr$ liquid metal RRBC \citep[]{chandrasekhar1961, zhang1997thermal, zhang2009onset, horn2017prograde, aurnou2018rotating .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Contrary to steady convection, which is subject to thermal boundary conditions, oscillatory convection is driven entirely by the internal temperature field.
elements.sent-2411-034: This thermally-driven inertial instability arises exclusively in rotating low $Pr<0.68$ fluids, like liquid metals and plasmas.
elements.sent-2411-035: The critical $Ra$ for which oscillatory modes onset (denoted as $Ra_O$, see Methods) occurs at lower $Ra$ than that for steady convection ($Ra_S$), such that thermal-inertial oscillations are considered the preferred mode of convection when the Prandtl number is sufficiently small \citep{zhang2017theory .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Here, by isolating the oscillatory mode in rotating liquid gallium ($Pr \approx 0.027$) convection experiments, we show that the inertial asymptotic DF scalings \eqref{eqn:DF_Nusselt - \eqref{eqn:DF_Theta can be realized in a desktop-sized RRBC cell, and are in good quantitative agreement with \citet{julien2012heat 's asymptotically-reduced RRBC models, further validating both laboratory and multi-scale asymptotic methods.}}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.975\linewidth]{figures/survey.png} \caption{Rotating convection survey measurements. (a) Global heat transport efficiency, $Nu$, and (b) vertical non-dimensional bulk velocity, $Re_z$, as a function of thermal buoyancy forcing, $Ra$. Symbol color denotes the Ekman number, $E$, where red (lowest $E$) indicates the strongest Coriolis forcing and purple (highest $E$) indicates weaker Coriolis forcing. Symbol shape denotes the cell aspect ratio, $\Gamma=D/H$. Laboratory results from this study ($\Gamma\simeq1/2$) are marked as circles, those from \citet[]{king2013turbulent} ($\Gamma\simeq1$) are marked as triangles, and those from \citet[]{vogt2021oscillatory} ($\Gamma\simeq2$) are marked as squares. Hollow points indicate cases in which the steady mode of convection has onset ($Ra\geq Ra_S$), which is used here as a transition point between inertial and steady convection regimes. The solid black line in each panel shows the non-rotating empirically-determined scaling for the $\Gamma\simeq1/2$ cell from this study for reference. A plot of the non-rotating RBC data used to generate these fits is shown in Supplementary Fig. \ref{fig:nonrotating}. The full data set can be found in Supplementary Table \ref{tab:data}.} \label{fig:survey} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-037: UCLA's RoMag device is used to conduct our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-038: The setup (Fig. \ref{fig:romag_main a and Supplementary Fig. \ref{fig:romag_supp a) features a right cylindrical convection cell with a height of $H=40.0$ cm and a diameter of $D=19.9$ cm, yielding an aspect ratio of $\Gamma=D/H\simeq1/2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: The cell is constructed with a stainless steel sidewall positioned between two copper end blocks.
elements.sent-2411-040: Heat is supplied through a basal heating pad, while a heat exchanger plate, connected to a re-circulating chiller, removes heat at the top.
elements.sent-2411-041: After equilibration, both the top and bottom copper end blocks are maintained at fixed temperatures for the duration of the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-042: The convection cell is affixed to a rotating platform that spins the cell about its vertical axis.
elements.sent-2411-043: We carry out a systematic survey, varying the Rayleigh and Ekman numbers by approximately two orders of magnitude each ($8\times10^6 < Ra < 1\times10^9$ and $ 3\times10^{-7 < E < 1\times10^{-5}$).}
elements.sent-2411-044: For each experiment, we acquire measurements of temperature, heat flux, and velocity.
elements.sent-2411-045: Thermistors embedded within the top and bottom thermal blocks measure the vertical temperature gradient, $\Delta T$ (see white lines in Fig. \ref{fig:romag_main b and pink/blue time series lines in Fig. \ref{fig:romag_main c).}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Five internal thermistors are positioned within the bulk fluid to measure the internal temperature fluctuations (see black lines in Fig. \ref{fig:romag_main b and black/gray time series lines in Fig. \ref{fig:romag_main c).}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The standard deviation of each internal thermistor time series is calculated to determine the local perturbation at each location, which are then RMS-averaged to yield $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-048: Vertical flow velocity, $u_z$, is measured using an ultrasonic Doppler velocimeter (UDV).
elements.sent-2411-049: A Doppler transducer probe is positioned in contact with the interior fluid, oriented along the vertical axis of the cylinder.
elements.sent-2411-050: Natural impurities in the gallium act as particles that the UDV can acoustically sense \citep{wang2021microscale .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Velocities are recorded at approximately 2 mm increments across the entire vertical length of the cell at a sampling rate of approximately 10 Hz.
elements.sent-2411-052: The characteristic $u_z$ velocity is then calculated as a time- and space-RMS, using the middle 50\% of the cell height (see orange lines and light orange area shaded in Fig. \ref{fig:romag_main b and the Hovmöller diagram in \ref{fig:romag_main c).}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The equations used for averaging are included in Methods.
elements.sent-2411-054: We compare our $\Gamma\simeq1/2$ convection cell results to those of two other aspect ratios from prior studies performed using the same experimental device.
elements.sent-2411-055: We additionally compare our findings to the asymptotically-reduced planar models of \citet[]{julien2012heat by incorporating their best-fit prefactor $C_J=1/25$ in the heat transfer prediction \eqref{eqn:DF_Nusselt .}}
elements.sent-2411-056: We elect to carry this prefactor through the asymptotic predictions for the Reynolds number \eqref{eqn:DF_Reynolds and thermal anomalies \eqref{eqn:DF_Theta , as shown in Methods.}}

elements.sent-2411-082: Overall, we find simultaneous agreement with diffusion-free predictions across all three independently measured quantities in this study: thermal anomalies in the bulk, velocities in the bulk, and global heat transfer.
elements.sent-2411-083: Further, we find increasing fidelity of this agreement in higher aspect ratio containers.
elements.sent-2411-084: These experiments show that diffusion-free physics can exist even in relatively small, closed containers of rotating fluid.
elements.sent-2411-085: Of specific note, we find that rotating low $Pr$ liquid metal convection in a short, wide aspect ratio tank ($\Gamma\simeq2$) robustly agrees with the varied $Pr$ results of the asymptotically-reduced planar modeling study of \citet{julien2012heat .}
elements.sent-2411-086: This work thus verifies the $C_J$ constant found by \citet{julien2012heat to hold for DF planar systems with no-slip top and bottom boundaries.}

elements.sent-2411-089: The laboratory-theoretical agreement crucially validates application of DF theory to natural settings.
elements.sent-2411-090: Here we consider Earth's outer core as an example.
elements.sent-2411-091: Scalings \eqref{eqn:DF_Nusselt - \eqref{eqn:DF_Theta are cast in terms of the superadiabatic temperature difference, $\Delta T$, where a known Rayleigh number or convective Rossby number is used to predict an unknown heat transfer, velocity, and thermal anomaly.}}
elements.sent-2411-092: However, the Rayleigh number is rarely known in geophysical or astrophysical applications; instead, a heat flux or flow velocity is typically estimated from observational data.
elements.sent-2411-093: Specifically for Earth's outer core, flow velocities are estimated from geomagnetic secular variation to be $u\simeq5\times10^{-4}$ m/s \citep{holme2015large , which gives a Reynolds number of $Re\simeq10^9$.}
elements.sent-2411-094: The diffusion-free Reynolds number equation \eqref{eqn:DF_Nusselt can then be converted to a flux-based format and rearranged to solve for the Rayleigh number as an unknown quantity: \begin{equation}Ra = C_J^{-2/5} Re E^{-1} Pr. \label{eqn:Ra_prediction_Re}\end{equation} Using \eqref{eqn:Ra_prediction_Re with $C_J=1/25$, $Re\simeq10^9$, $E\simeq10^{-15}$, and $Pr\simeq0.1$ for Earth's outer core, the Rayleigh number is calculated to be $Ra\simeq4\times10^{23}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-095: The Rayleigh number estimate can then be used to determine the superadiabatic temperature differential $\Delta T$ in the core following $\Delta T = Ra \cdot \nu \kappa / (\alpha g_{cmb H^3) \simeq 3$ mK, where $\nu\simeq10^{-6}$ m$^2$/s, $\kappa\simeq10^{-5}$ m$^2$/s, $\alpha\simeq1.2\times10^{-5}$ 1/K, $g_{cmb}\simeq10.68$ m/s, and $H\simeq2255$ km \citep{olson2015core .}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Using \eqref{eqn:DF_Theta , the temperature anomaly is calculated as $\theta = (1/25)^{3/5 (RaE^2Pr^{-1 )^{1/2 \Delta T \simeq 10^{-6}$ K.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-102: Measurements of the inertial oscillation-dominated range of liquid metal RRBC in closed cylindrical laboratory containers reveal that asymptotic DF heat and momentum transfer can be realized on the laboratory scale.
elements.sent-2411-103: We observe these trends in measurements of internal flow velocities and thermal fluctuations regardless of tank geometry.
elements.sent-2411-104: In contrast, global heat transfer is significantly influenced by wall modes in narrow, low aspect ratio cells, with asymptotic trends predominantly found in wide, high aspect ratio cells that better resemble plane layer geometry.
elements.sent-2411-105: If wall modes can be suppressed \citep[cf.][]{terrien2023suppression , it may be possible to achieve asymptotic heat transfer scalings in taller, more slender $\Gamma \lesssim 1$ tanks.}
elements.sent-2411-106: It may be possible to further investigate DF scaling behaviors using lower $Pr$ fluids, such as liquid sodium, in which $Ra_S/Ra_O$ attain larger values.
elements.sent-2411-107: By verifying here that diffusivity-free convection exists in a standard desktop-scale RRBC experimental cell, it is now possible to accurately and confidently model the convective turbulence that exists in rapidly rotating planetary and stellar interiors.

elements.sent-2411-110: The laboratory device used here, RoMag, has been used in a number of previous liquid metal convection studies \citep[]{king2013turbulent, king2015magnetostrophic, aurnou2018rotating, vogt2018jump, vogt2021oscillatory, grannan2022experimental, xu2022thermoelectric, xu2023transition , two of which produced results included in Fig. \ref{fig:survey and Fig. \ref{fig:asymptotic for analysis \citep[]{king2015magnetostrophic, vogt2021oscillatory .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: For the new data acquired in this study, the setup consists of a right cylindrical cell of inner diameter $D=2R=19.68$ cm and height $H=40$ cm to yield an aspect ratio of $\Gamma=D/H=0.49$.
elements.sent-2411-112: The cell sidewall is made of stainless steel, the top and bottom plate boundaries are made of copper, and the working fluid is liquid gallium ($Pr\approx0.027$).
elements.sent-2411-113: Layers of aerogel insulation (approximately 3 cm thick total) surround the cell sidewall, while a 7.62 cm thick calcium silicate insulation block sits below the lower plate boundary to mitigate heat losses.
elements.sent-2411-114: A non-inductively wound electrical heating pad at the bottom of the cell supplies a fixed flux of heating power ranging from $P = 10$ to 2000 W, while the top of the cell is maintained at a fixed temperature using a recirculating chiller.
elements.sent-2411-115: The generated temperature gradients range from approximately $\Delta T = 1$ to 60 K.
elements.sent-2411-116: The cell is mounted on a platform that rotates at rates ranging from $\Omega=0.105$ to $\Omega= 3.67$ rad/s (1 to 35 revolutions per minute).
elements.sent-2411-117: A systematic survey was performed such that the Rayleigh and Ekman numbers each vary by approximately two orders of magnitude ($8\times10^6 < Ra < 1\times10^9$ and $ 3\times10^{-7 < E < 1\times10^{-5}$).. \ Data acquisition.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Twelve thermistors are located within the top and bottom plate boundaries to measure the global temperature gradient, \begin{equation}\Delta T = \overline{T}_{bot}-\overline{T}_{top},\end{equation} which is used in the calculation of the Rayleigh and Nusselt numbers \citep[e.g.,][]{king2012heat, xu2022thermoelectric, abbate2023rotating .}
elements.sent-2411-119: Six thermistors contribute to each the top and bottom boundary temperature calculation following \begin{equation}\overline{T}_{top, \ bot} = \frac{1}{6N_t} \sum_{i=1}^{6} \sum_{j=1}^{N_t} T_{ij} \ \pm \ \frac{\overline{q} d} {k_{Cu}} , \label{eqn:temperature}\end{equation} where $\overline{T _{top}$ is determined using only sensors in the top plate, $\overline{T _{bot}$ is determined from those in the bottom, and $N_t$ is the length of the time series.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: The thermistors are embedded within the copper plates at a location $d=2$ mm away from the fluid-plate interface, so the measured temperature is adjusted to correct for conduction over length $d$ via the second term in \eqref{eqn:temperature , where $\overline{q}$ is the time-averaged heat flux given by \eqref{eqn:q_final and $k_{Cu}$ is the thermal conductivity of the copper plate.}}
elements.sent-2411-121: The temperature-dependent fluid material properties are calculated using the mean temperature, \begin{equation}\overline{T} = (\overline{T}_{bot}+\overline{T}_{top})/2 .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-122: The input heat flux, $q_{in =P_{in /A$, is set by the power supply where $P_{in}$ is the wattage of the input heating power and $A$ is the active surface area of the fluid-plate interface.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: This value is confirmed with measurements of current, $I$, and voltage, $V$, at the location of the heating pad to calculate $P_{in =IV$.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Thermistors located within the layers of aerogel insulation are used to measure the heat lost through the cell sidewall.
elements.sent-2411-125: This loss is calculated as \begin{equation}P_{sw,loss} = \frac{2 \pi k_{ins} H (T_{sw} - T_{ins})}{\log(R_{ins}/R_{sw})} ,\end{equation} where $k_{ins}$ is the thermal conductivity of aerogel, $T_{sw}$ is the temperature measured at cell sidewall, $T_{ins}$ is the temperature measured after one layer of insulation, $R_{sw}$ is the radial position of the sidewall thermistor, and $R_{ins}$ is the radial position of the insulation thermistor.
elements.sent-2411-126: This loss is subtracted from the power measured at the location of the heating pad.
elements.sent-2411-127: Some power is additionally lost to vertical conduction through the stainless steel sidewall.
elements.sent-2411-128: This is estimated via \begin{equation}P_{sw,cond} = \frac{\pi (R_{sw}^2-R^2) k_{sw} \Delta T}{H} ,\end{equation} where $R$ is the inner radius of the cylinder and $k_{sw}$ is the thermal conductivity of the stainless steel sidewall.
elements.sent-2411-129: The total heat flux through the fluid is thus \begin{equation}\overline{q} = \frac{\overline{P}_{in} - \overline{P}_{sw,loss} - \overline{P}_{sw,cond}}{\pi R^2} , \label{eqn:q_final}\end{equation} where each overlined quantity is time-averaged.
elements.sent-2411-130: This $\overline{q}$ is then used in the calculation of the Nusselt number.
elements.sent-2411-131: Five internal thermistors are located within the fluid interior of the cell to measure bulk temperature fluctuations, $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-132: Internal thermistor locations are shown in Fig. \ref{fig:romag_main b and Supplementary Fig. \ref{fig:romag_supp b.}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Each internal thermistor measures the local temperature at its respective location over the course of the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-134: The standard deviation of the temperature time series provides the thermal fluctuation at that location (Fig. \ref{fig:romag_main c).}
elements.sent-2411-135: The root-mean-square (rms) value of the five thermistors' measured fluctuations is calculated to estimate the characteristic temporally and spatially averaged temperature fluctuation, $\theta$, in the convecting fluid bulk.
elements.sent-2411-136: The equation is thus given as \begin{equation}\theta = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{5N_t} \sum_{i=1}^{5}\sum_{j=1}^{N_t} (T_{i,j}-\overline{T}_i)^2 } .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-137: This value is then normalized by $\Delta T$ in our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-138: The vertical flow velocity, $u_z$, is measured using an ultrasonic Doppler velocimeter (UDV).
elements.sent-2411-139: For these experiments, three vertically oriented transducer probes are embedded within the top thermal block of the convection cell, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:romag_main b and Supplementary Fig. \ref{fig:romag_supp c.}}
elements.sent-2411-140: They are each in direct contact with the liquid gallium, which contains oxides that are acoustically sensed by the UDV \citep{wang2021microscale .}
elements.sent-2411-141: The UDV velocities are recorded at approximately 2 mm increments across the entire vertical length of the cell at an approximately 10 Hz date rate \citep{vogt2018jump, vogt2021oscillatory ; see Fig. \ref{fig:romag_main b.}}
elements.sent-2411-142: The characteristic $u_z$ velocity is calculated as a spatiotemporal rms, using UDV data from the middle 50\% of the cell height ($h = 0.25H$ to 0.75$H$) (Fig. \ref{fig:romag_main ), following \begin{equation}u_z = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{N_z N_t} \sum_{i=1}^{N_z} \sum_{j=1}^{N_t} u_{i,j}^2 } \ ,\end{equation} where $u_{i,j}$ is each velocity measurement at space element $i$ and time element $j$.}

elements.sent-2411-144: Convective instabilities arise in a number of different ways in rotating systems.
elements.sent-2411-145: Here, three modes of convection are relevant to the experiments conducted.
elements.sent-2411-146: The primary mode is known as the steady or stationary mode.
elements.sent-2411-147: This type of instability occurs when thermal buoyancy overcomes the stabilizing effect of rotation, such that fluid warmed by bottom boundary rises, and fluid cooled by the top boundary sinks.
elements.sent-2411-148: This mode is therefore fueled by thermal fluxes through the boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-149: The critical value of $Ra$ for which steady convection onsets is dependent on the Ekman number following \begin{equation}Ra_S^{\infty} = 8.7 E^{-4/3}\end{equation} in a horizontal plane layer in the limit of infinitely low $E$ \citep{chandrasekhar1961, aurnou2018rotating , or \begin{equation}\label{eqn:steady} Ra_S = (8.7 - 9.63 E^{1/6}) E^{-4/3}\end{equation} for a planar system rotating at a finite rate ($E > 0$) and no-slip top and bottom boundaries \citep[]{niiler1965influence, kunnen2021geostrophic .}}
elements.sent-2411-150: In this work, expression \eqref{eqn:steady is employed.}
elements.sent-2411-151: In systems with lateral boundaries, convective instabilities additionally develop at the sidewall in the form of waves of alternating upward and downward flow that travel around the perimeter of the cell.
elements.sent-2411-152: These are known as wall modes.
elements.sent-2411-153: For a no-slip, thermally insulated system, wall modes are predicted to onset at a critical Rayleigh number given by \begin{equation}\label{eqn:wall} Ra_W = \pi^2 \sqrt{6 \sqrt{3}} E^{-1} + 46.55 E^{-2/3}\end{equation} \citep{zhang2009onset .}
elements.sent-2411-154: These modes are strongest at the periphery of the tank and aren't expected to significantly affect the bulk interior flow quantities (i.e., $u_z$, $\theta$).
elements.sent-2411-155: However, they do contribute to the total global heat transport ($Nu$) for the system.
elements.sent-2411-156: Specific to rotating fluids with $Pr < 0.68$, convective instability can also develop in the form of thermally driven inertial oscillations known as oscillatory modes \citep{zhang1997thermal .}
elements.sent-2411-157: This instability onsets at a lower $Ra$ than the critical value for steady modes.
elements.sent-2411-158: The prediction for onset in a horizontally infinite plane layer follows \begin{equation}\label{eqn:oscillatory} Ra_O^{\infty} = 17.4 \left( \frac{E}{Pr} \right)^{-4/3}\end{equation} \citep{chandrasekhar1961 .}
elements.sent-2411-159: Oscillatory onset predictions in finite cylindrical geometry subject to non-slip experimental boundary conditions, $Ra_O^{cyl}$, are given in \citet{zhang2009onset , verified through comparisons with simulations at $E = 10^{-4 $, $\Gamma = 2$ and $10^{-6 \leq Pr \leq 0.05$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-160: In contrast to steady and wall modes, which are directly linked to the system boundaries, oscillatory modes are driven by the internal temperature gradient, and are therefore independent of the boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-161: Isolating the oscillatory mode can uniquely enable convection that is free from diffusive boundary effects.
elements.sent-2411-162: Taking the ratio of the critical onset prediction for the stationary mode to that of the oscillatory mode gives \begin{equation}\label{eqn:onset_ratio_steady} \frac{Ra_S^{\infty}}{Ra_O^{\infty}} = \frac{1}{2} Pr^{-4/3} .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-163: Equation \eqref{eqn:onset_ratio_steady shows that the smaller the Prandtl number is, the larger the separation between the onset of each mode.}
elements.sent-2411-164: Liquid gallium, at $Pr\simeq0.027$, yields $Ra_S^{\infty /Ra_O^{\infty}\simeq60$, so we can access oscillatory-dominated convection for approximately 1-2 orders of magnitude in the Rayleigh number.}
elements.sent-2411-165: Fluids with smaller Prandtl numbers, such as liquid sodium with $Pr\simeq0.001$, would provide an even larger range in which oscillatory convection can be studied with $Ra_S^{\infty /Ra_O^{\infty}\simeq10^3$, yielding approximately 3 orders of magnitude in $Ra$ before steady convection onsets.}
elements.sent-2411-166: The ratio of the wall mode onset prediction to that of the oscillatory mode simplifies to \begin{equation}\label{eqn:onset_ratio_wall} \frac{Ra_W}{Ra_O^{\infty}} = ( 1.83 E^{1/3} + 2.74 E^{2/3} ) \ Pr^{-4/3} .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-167: According to \eqref{eqn:onset_ratio_wall , the Ekman number needs to be moderately large to isolate oscillatory modes from wall modes in low $Pr$ fluids.}
elements.sent-2411-168: This could be achieved with relatively slow rotation, or preferably, with relatively small $H$, which would additionally aid in keeping $Ra$ below $Ra_S$.

elements.sent-2411-180: The asymptotic scaling prediction for diffusivity-free heat transfer in rotating convection is \begin{equation}Nu-1 = C_J \ Ra^{3/2} E^{2} Pr^{-1/2}, \label{eqn:HXDF}\end{equation} where $C_J \approx 1/25$ is a constant estimated in the asymptotically-reduced modeling study of \citet[]{julien2012heat .}
elements.sent-2411-181: Non-diffusive momentum transfer is determined from a triple balance between the Coriolis, inertia, and buoyancy terms of the vorticity equation, defining a ‘CIA’ prediction for the Reynolds number that yields \begin{equation}Re = C_1 \left( \frac{Ra (Nu-1)}{Pr^2} \right)^{2/5} E^{1/5} \label{eqn:ReCIA}\end{equation} \citep[]{ingersoll1982motion, cardin1994chaotic, aubert2001systematic, king2013scaling, guervilly2019turbulent, aurnou2020connections, kolhey2022influence, oliver2023small, hawkins2023laboratory, abbate2023rotating .}
elements.sent-2411-182: Further, CIA scaling arguments \citep{aubert2001systematic, abbate2023rotating also yield predictions for the diffusion-free rotating convective temperature perturbations in the fluid bulk, $\theta$, as \begin{equation}\frac{\theta}{\Delta T} = C_2 \left(\frac{Nu-1}{RaE}\right)^{3/5} Ro_c^{2/5} . \label{eqn:ThetaCIA}\end{equation} Substituting the asymptotic heat transfer scaling \eqref{eqn:HXDF into the CIA predictions \eqref{eqn:ReCIA and \eqref{eqn:ThetaCIA yields estimates for the nondimensional velocity, $Re$, and the nondimensional temperature perturbation, $\theta/\Delta T$, in the asymptotic, diffusivity-free regime: \begin{equation}Re = C_1 C_J^{2/5} Ro_c^{2}E^{-1}, \label{eqn:DF_Re_Supp}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\frac{\theta}{\Delta T} = C_2 C_J^{3/5} Ro_c . \label{eqn:DF_Theta_Supp}\end{equation} Substituting $Re=Ro/E$ into the Reynolds number prediction yields \begin{equation}Ro = C_1 C_J^{2/5} Ro_c^{2}, \label{eqn:DF_Ro_Supp}\end{equation} where $Ro=u/(2\Omega H)$ is the Rossby number.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: Interestingly, the asymptotic scalings \eqref{eqn:DF_Theta_Supp and \eqref{eqn:DF_Ro_Supp both depend only on the convective Rossby number, $Ro_c = (Ra E^2/Pr)^{1/2}$ \citep[cf.~][]{aurnou2020connections, abbate2023rotating .}}}
elements.sent-2411-184: Setting $C_J \approx 1/25$ in \eqref{eqn:HXDF , $C_J^{2/5 \approx (1/25)^{2/5 = 0.276$ in \eqref{eqn:DF_Re_Supp , $C_J^{3/5 \approx (1/25)^{3/5 = 0.145$ in \eqref{eqn:DF_Theta_Supp , and assuming $C_1 \approx C_2 \approx 1$ for simplicity, we arrive at the quantitative asymptotically reduced modeling predictions for the heat transfer, momentum transfer, and bulk thermal anomalies, respectively, against which we compare our laboratory liquid metal rotating convection data in Fig. \ref{fig:asymptotic and Table \ref{tab:bestfits .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-185: Our results in Table \ref{tab:bestfits show that actual values are $C_1=1.50\pm0.58$ and $C_2=2.69\pm0.35$ for the $\Gamma\simeq1/2$ tank, and $C_1=2.21\pm0.88$ and $C_2=1.46\pm0.16$ for the $\Gamma\simeq2$ tank, which are consistent with order unity.}
elements.sent-2411-186: In this work, we have used a $\Delta T$-based framework, but the thermal flux is often what is most easily measured in most geophysical and astrophysical cases.
elements.sent-2411-187: In this case, the diffusivity-free heat transfer prediction can instead be used to predict the Rayleigh number, $Ra$, from a given flux-based Rayleigh number, $Ra_F$.

elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth]{figures/experiment_supp.png} \caption{Experimental apparatus. (a) Rotating convection cell. The cylindrical tank is $H$ = 40.0 cm tall by $D$ = 19.68 cm wide (aspect ratio = $\Gamma=D/H\approx1/2$) and filled with liquid gallium ($Pr\approx0.027$). Heat is supplied from a non-inductively wound heating pad through the bottom copper `heating' block, and extracted by a heat exchanger plate in contact with the top copper `cooling' block, to generate axially-aligned thermal buoyancy-driven convection. The cell is mounted on a rotating pedestal to rotate the cell about its vertical axis. An insulation blanket surrounds the cell to mitigate heat losses. (b) Underside of the top copper `cooling block'. Five internal thermistors of varied lengths are positioned within the fluid to measure internal temperatures. elements.figure-2411-008: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{figures/transitions.png} \caption{Tests of transition values at which scaling behavior deviates from the diffusion-free predictions. elements.sent-2411-192: Convection in planets and stars is predicted to occur in the ``ultimate regime'' of diffusivity-free, rapidly rotating turbulence, in which flows are characteristically unaffected by viscous and thermal diffusion.
elements.sent-2411-193: We triply verify the existence of the diffusivity-free regime via measurements of heat transfer efficiency $Nu$, dimensionless flow velocities $Re$, and internal temperature anomalies $\theta$, all of which are in quantitative agreement with planar asymptotically-reduced models.

elements.sent-2411-194: Diffusivity-free (DF) turbulent rotating convection is theorized to occur in the convection zones of stars and the fluid cores of planets, where it drives vortical flows, jets, and the dynamo action that sustains global-scale magnetic fields \citep{moffatt2019self .}
elements.sent-2411-195: The DF regime is characterized by strong turbulence that is dominated by fluid inertia, and is unaffected by the fluid's micro-scale viscosity ($\nu$) and thermal diffusivity ($\kappa$).
elements.sent-2411-196: Fluid physics researchers have long sought to validate this theoretical regime through measurements of heat transfer efficiency in numerical simulations and laboratory experiments \citep[e.g.,][]{ahlers2009heat, ecke2023turbulent .}
elements.sent-2411-197: It remains an essential question whether the theorized DF regime can be attained in a laboratory setting and extrapolated to natural settings.

elements.subsection-2411-001: Fundamental Scalings of Diffusivity-Free Rotating Convection
elements.sent-2411-223: The diffusivity-free (DF) scaling prediction for superadiabatic heat transfer in rotating convection follows \begin{equation}Nu_{conv} \sim \ Ra^{3/2} E^{2} Pr^{-1/2} \label{eqn:HXDF}\end{equation} \cite[cf.][]{gillet2006quasi, julien2012heat .}
elements.sent-2411-224: The subscript `conv' denotes the superadiabatic, or `convective', quantity such that \begin{equation}Nu_{conv} = \frac{q_{conv}}{q_{cond}} \ ,\end{equation} where $q_{conv}$ and $q_{cond}$ represent the convective heat flux and heat lost to conduction, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-225: These quantities are related to the total Nusselt number, $Nu$, following \begin{equation}\label{eqn:Nusselt} Nu = \frac{q_{total}}{q_{cond}} = \frac{q_{cond} +q_{conv}}{q_{cond}} = 1 + \frac{q_{conv}}{q_{cond}} \ ,\end{equation} implying that $Nu - 1 = Nu_{conv}$.
elements.sent-2411-226: In systems where $Nu\gg1$, it is usually assumed that $Nu-1 \approx Nu$ and therefore $Nu_{conv \approx Nu$, but this assumption is not valid near the onset of convection when $Nu$ is small, as is the case in this study.}

elements.sent-2411-243: Substituting the asymptotic heat transfer scaling \eqref{eqn:HXDF_2 into the CIA predictions, \eqref{eqn:ReCIA and \eqref{eqn:ThetaCIA , yields estimates for the nondimensional velocity, $Re$, and the nondimensional temperature perturbation, $\theta/\Delta T$, in the asymptotic, diffusivity-free regime: \begin{equation} Re = C_1 C_J^{2/5} Ro_c^{2}E^{-1} \ , \label{eqn:DF_Re_Supp}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\frac{\theta}{\Delta T} = C_2 C_J^{3/5} Ro_c \ . \label{eqn:DF_Theta_Supp}\end{equation} Substituting $Re=Ro/E$ into the Reynolds number prediction yields \begin{equation}Ro = C_1 C_J^{2/5} Ro_c^{2} \ , \label{eqn:DF_Ro_Supp}\end{equation} where $Ro=u/(2\Omega H)$ is the Rossby number.}}}
